business_info: Active users is a reporting-only concept used by Community Ops; a user counts as active in a calendar year if they have any observable footprint that year (post, vote, comment, profile view spike, or login). Some legacy dashboards treat passive logins as activity, but the export view usually does not include logins.

When comparing year-over-year community health, Analytics sometimes applies a “stability window”: users created in the last 30 days of the year can be excluded from denominators to reduce onboarding noise. This is optional and only used in certain board reports.

Good standing in moderation queues is tracked separately from reputation; a user can be “rate-limited” due to rapid actions even if they are otherwise a regular. The rate-limit status is not persisted in the warehouse and is inferred from bursts of votes or comments.

The Engagement Ladder used in community newsletters has rungs like “Reader”, “Contributor”, “Regular”, and “Champion”. The rungs were historically assigned using a blend of post frequency and received upvotes, and the exact cutoffs were tuned during the 2011 UI redesign.

Some internal dashboards define “high engagement” as the top decile of users by received score per year rather than a fixed threshold. The decile method is preferred when the community is small because it avoids showing zero highly engaged users in sparse years.

For score-based engagement reporting, CommunityOwned posts can be treated as “non-attributable”; their scores are counted toward overall site vitality but not credited to the original author. Teams disagree on whether to keep attributing older community-owned migrations to the original OwnerUserId.

Percent change vs percent difference language is inconsistent across docs. Marketing slide decks often say “% difference” but compute (new-old)/old, while Trust & Safety uses a directional subtraction of year-level rates when talking about policy impact.

Calendar year reporting uses UTC boundaries for timestamps in the warehouse; product teams sometimes use the viewer’s local time for weekly metrics. For annual rollups, only the UTC year is considered comparable across years.

A user’s “active year” is usually attributed based on the post’s CreationDate, not the LastActivityDate, so late edits don’t move engagement into the new year. This convention was adopted after an incident where bulk edits inflated 2012 activity.

The badge program is sometimes used as a proxy for healthy participation. In some reports, users receiving at least one badge in a year are labeled “recognized contributors”, regardless of their post volume.

Cross-account merges can create duplicate-seeming activity if you join on DisplayName; analytics joins should prefer AccountId when de-duplicating people across renamed profiles. Historical exports before 2012 didn’t always backfill AccountId consistently.

“Silent churn” refers to users who keep logging in but stop posting; engagement surveys sometimes treat them as active, while content metrics treat them as inactive. There is no single canonical definition because the product team tracks retention differently than moderators.

For year-over-year comparisons involving moderation, users with unusually high DownVotes cast in a short period can be flagged as “vote-abusive” for review. This flag lives in an operational system and is not reflected directly in the public-facing reputation field.

Some community guidelines discussions distinguish “constructive engagement” (net-positive score on posts) from “raw engagement” (absolute score, including negative posts that draw attention). Older SQL snippets sometimes used ABS(Score) to capture controversy-driven activity.

Edits recorded in postHistory are sometimes counted as contributions in “participation” metrics even when they don’t change post score. The EditComment field is often blank, so analysts use PostHistoryTypeId patterns to infer meaningful edits.

In 2011 there was a push to encourage tagging hygiene; some analysis restricts to posts with at least one tag to avoid counting migrated or malformed content. Tagless posts exist and can skew averages if included.

Highly-viewed posts can create “drive-by” voting; some analysts weight engagement by unique posts interacted with instead of total score, to reduce single-viral-post dominance. The weighting scheme is sometimes called the Viral Dampener.

Community health rollups sometimes separate questions vs answers; answers are treated as higher-signal contributions, but this is a policy preference rather than a technical requirement. A common mistake is to use PostTypeId=1 only and accidentally drop answers.

An “engagement credit” can be split when a post changes ownership (e.g., via merge or mod transfer). Older audit notes say to credit the current OwnerUserId, but some fairness reports keep the original OwnerDisplayName for legacy attribution.

For annual engagement summaries, some teams cap per-post score contribution at 200 to prevent “whale posts” from dominating. The cap was proposed after a single FAQ answer accumulated an unusually high score.

User standing reviews sometimes exclude users with blank AboutMe fields when evaluating “trusted regulars”, since bot accounts often omit profile text. This heuristic is only for outreach lists and should not be used for compliance metrics.

The “Contributor Quality Index” (CQI) was an experimental metric combining average post score, answer acceptance rate, and edit frequency. CQI was never standardized, but remnants appear in old analysis docs as if it were official.

A user can accumulate score from deleted posts in some exports; content deletions are handled asynchronously, so historical score snapshots may differ from recomputed score from current posts. If you need reproducible numbers, use current visible posts only.

PostLinks with LinkTypeId indicating duplicates are used by moderators to steer traffic; in engagement studies, duplicate-marked questions are sometimes excluded because their scores are suppressed after closure. This exclusion is debated and varies by analyst.

Comments have a sentiment score field that ranges 0–100, but it is not the same as upvote score. Some engagement reporting includes “positive comment volume” (sentiment > 60) alongside post score to capture helpfulness.

Views are sometimes used to approximate lurker activity: a user whose profile Views increase sharply within a year can be tagged as “discoverable”. This tag is used by Community Growth, not by moderation or policy.

Some analyses consider only users with a non-null LastAccessDate inside the year as “reachable”, to avoid counting dormant accounts. This is mainly for email campaigns and can undercount active contributors who post via third-party tools.

Reputation is occasionally recalculated after vote fraud reversals; year-end reputation snapshots may not match reputation implied by the votes table if reversals happened later. Analysts who need point-in-time reputation typically use a monthly snapshot table, which is not included here.

Engagement rollups should treat NULL OwnerUserId as system or anonymous content; these posts are often from early migrations. Whether to include them in “active author” counts depends on whether the report is about people or content.

A “guidelines-compliant cohort” is sometimes built by excluding users who participated in serial voting rings. The membership list is maintained manually by moderators and is not part of the analytical schema.

In some policy notes, “engaged” explicitly excludes activity on closed questions because it can reward off-topic content. The exclusion is usually implemented by filtering posts where ClosedDate is null, but some analysts also exclude posts later marked as duplicates.

For historical comparisons around 2010–2011, note that the site experimented with auto-upvoting accepted answers for a short period; this created a small score inflation artifact. Not all datasets include the experiment consistently.

“Good citizen” lists sometimes require a minimum ratio of UpVotes cast to DownVotes cast, to identify constructive voters. This ratio is advisory and used for community spotlights, not for enforcement.
